text: The surgeon begins the recurrent left inguinal hernia repair by identifying the thick, rolled-up mesh from the previous procedure. The peritoneum overlying the mesh appears very thin. The surgeon carefully dissects around the mesh, aiming to avoid critical structures, particularly the inferior epigastric vessels, which are partially visible. Cautery is used liberally to maintain hemostasis and prevent staining of the tissue. The sliding hernia with colon is noted, with the mesh positioned away from the colon, allowing safe use of cautery down to the posterior edge of the mesh. Attention is given to avoid damaging nerves such as the lateral femoral cutaneous and femoral branch.

The surgeon works in the midline suprapubic area, using cautery liberally to maintain hemostasis while dissecting avascular planes. The left hand applies tension to the tissue, aiding in precise division with scissors. The surgeon identifies areolar tissue stuck to the pubis and carefully snips it close to the periosteum. Firefly technology is prepared to visualize the inferior epigastric vessels using ICG injection.

The surgeon encounters the transversus abdominis muscle and begins working deeper towards the peritoneum or hernia sac. They identify a layer that moves, indicating a potential avascular plane for dissection. The anesthesiologist administers ICG, and the fibrogastric vessels are observed lighting up under Firefly imaging. The surgeon uses cautery on the mesh with tension applied to separate tissue, while monitoring for blood vessels.

The surgeon carefully dissects the sac from the internal ring, using the left hand to protect the colon and lift the tissue with scissors. Cautery is applied anteriorly where the sac is devoid of colon. The surgeon moves slowly, identifying areas where the mesh is tethered, and dissects into the retropubic space with a focus on hemostasis. The areolar tissue is distinguished from the bladder, and short bursts of cautery are used to minimize bleeding around small vessels.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to focus energy on non-bloody tissue in the retropubic space, identifying a blue proline suture used to anchor the mesh. The scissors bounce off the mesh as the surgeon carefully scores near the inferior epigastric vessels, ready to apply pressure if bleeding occurs. The surgeon attempts to locate the end of the hernia sac to separate it from cord structures, cautiously exposing and thinning the tissue while considering the vas deferens.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, focusing on the dissection of the hernia sac and mesh. A hole is visible in the sac, aiding in the identification of surrounding structures and ensuring the bowel is not compromised. The surgeon carefully separates the mesh from the inferior epigastric vessels, avoiding injury to the iliac vessels. The use of bipolar cautery is evident, providing strong retraction and precise cauterization of tissue adhered to the mesh.

The surgeon carefully cauterizes on the mesh side and pushes the tissue off, revealing a layer of scar tissue between the mesh and critical structures. The tissue coming off the mesh resembles a capsule, providing reassurance that the critical structures are several cell layers away. The surgeon continues to dissect the mesh from the surrounding tissue, maintaining good hemostasis and visual clarity. The obturator space is approached, and the mesh is transitioned from the deep retropubic space onto the psoas muscle for optimal coverage.

The surgeon completes the dissection around the vas deferens, ensuring the mesh covers the area without the bladder or peritoneum sneaking underneath. The medial umbilical ligament is visible but not divided due to sufficient exposure. The surgeon confirms more than two centimeters of coverage into the retropubic space, with the old mesh well integrated and a hole in the peritoneum to be closed with a 3-0 V-Lock 180 suture. Retroperitoneal fat is separated from the testicular vessels to prevent hernia recurrence. The genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve are demonstrated, protected by investing fascia. The surgeon plans to use Progrip mesh for better fixation, ensuring the posterior edge does not lift.

The surgeon ensures the bladder and hernia fat cannot protrude by unraveling and positioning a large 16x12 anatomical mesh piece with adequate overlap in all directions. The mesh is tucked securely to prevent any lead points from the hernia fat sneaking underneath.
